items.q: What is the doctrine of the Trinity?
items.correct: God is one being existing in three persons (Father, Son, Holy Spirit) — distinct in person but united in essence; Christians remain monotheistic.
items.misconception: Christians believe in three different gods.
items.random_wrong: The Trinity refers to heaven, earth and hell.
items.why: Students treat 'three persons' as 'three gods'. The orthodox position is one God in three distinct persons sharing one substance — a notoriously difficult concept students often misrepresent as polytheism.

items.q: What do orthodox Christians believe about the nature of Jesus?
items.correct: He is fully God and fully human at the same time (the incarnation) — both natures united in one person, as defined at the Council of Chalcedon.
items.misconception: Jesus was half God and half human.
items.random_wrong: Jesus was a human who became God after his resurrection.
items.why: The 'half and half' model was historically rejected as heresy. Orthodox doctrine holds both natures fully and simultaneously — a deliberately paradoxical claim students often soften.

items.q: Is the Buddha a god in Buddhism?
items.correct: No — the Buddha (Siddhartha Gautama) is regarded as an enlightened human teacher who discovered the path to liberation. Theravada Buddhism is generally non-theistic; some Mahayana traditions develop more elaborate cosmologies.
items.misconception: Buddhists worship the Buddha as their god.
items.why: Statues and offerings look like worship to students, but classically the Buddha is venerated as teacher, not worshipped as creator god. The path he taught, not his divinity, is what matters in most Buddhist traditions.

items.q: Which theodicy was developed by John Hick?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Soul-making. “Free will defence” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which book did Richard Dawkins publish in 2006 attacking religion?
items.why: It's The God Delusion. “The Selfish Gene” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was Dawkins' 'The God Delusion' first published?
items.why: Exact answer: 2006. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
